title: Vincetoxicum parvum
intro: Vincetoxicum parvum, also known as Small Vincetoxicum or Small Milkweed Orchid, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Apocynaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its purported health benefits, including the treatment of cardiovascular diseases, as it is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help lower blood pressure and cholesterol levels. The bioactive constituents of Vincetoxicum parvum include vincetoxin, a diterpenoid lignan, and other alkaloids, which are thought to be responsible for its medicinal effects. In traditional herbal medicine, Vincetoxicum parvum is commonly prepared as a decoction or tincture, and is often used to make teas, capsules, and infusions for the treatment of various health conditions.
taxonomy: Vincetoxicum parvum is classified within the family Apocynaceae. This species is a type of milkweed vine native to Eastern Asia. It is further categorized under the genus Vincetoxicum. More specifically, it belongs to the subgenus Vincetoxicum. Additionally, Vincetoxicum parvum is also known as Asclepias parvula.

constituents: Vincetoxicum parvum. The active constituents of Vincetoxicum parvum, also known as the small swallowwort, include vincetoxine and rotenone, which are toxic compounds. side_effects: Vincetoxicum parvum is a plant that may cause side effects due to its toxic compounds. These compounds, known as cardenolides, can be harmful if ingested or if they come into contact with the skin. Ingestion of Vincetoxicum parvum can cause symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Prolonged exposure to the plant's toxic compounds can also lead to more severe health issues, including cardiac problems and seizures. It is essential to handle the plant with caution and avoid ingesting it to prevent adverse reactions.
